subject: Mathematics

unit.unitOverview: This guide is written for the teacher or parent who will lead a young child (age 5–6) through Unit 1. Year 1 learners are usually pre-readers, so this unit is taught with you, not read alone by the child. Your role is to talk, show, and play alongside the child using the simple scripts and activities in this guide. The child's own work happens in the companion Activity Sheet and Practice documents.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, activities and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Mathematics 0096 — Stage 1 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Count a group of up to 10 objects accurately, touching each one.
Say the number names in order from 0 to 10.
Recognise and read the written numbers 0 to 10.
Write the digits 0 to 10 with correct formation.
Say how many without counting for very small groups (1–3).
Estimate roughly how many, then count to check.
Compare two groups and say which has more or fewer.
Put numbers in order and find a missing number in a track.
A handful of small countable objects: dates, dry beans, stones, bottle caps, or buttons (at least 20).

concepts.title: Counting Real Objects (one-to-one)
concepts.explanation: The single most important idea is one-to-one counting: one number name for each object, touched once. Many young children ‘count’ by rushing the words without matching them to objects. Slow, touched counting fixes this. How to teach it: Place 5 dates in a row. Take the child's finger in yours and touch each date as you both say ‘one, two, three, four, five.’ Then say, ‘So how many dates altogether?’ If the child recounts instead of answering ‘five’, gently repeat – understanding that the last number tells the…
concepts.example: 🗣 Suggested dialogue You: “Let us count the goats together. Touch each one. Ready?” Child and you: “One… two… three… four… five.” You: “How many goats altogether?” Child: “Five!” You: “Yes! The last number tells us how…

concepts.title: Ten-Frames
concepts.explanation: A ten-frame is a simple 2×5 grid. Filling it with counters helps the child ‘see’ a number as a shape rather than counting from one every time. Fill the top row first (left to right), then the bottom row. How to teach it: Give the child an egg carton cut to ten cups, or draw a ten-frame. Ask them to place 3 beans, then 6, then 10. Ask, ‘When you put 6 beans, how many empty cups are left?’ This quietly plants early ideas of number bonds to ten, which they will meet fully in Unit 5.
concepts.example: Encourage the child to fill the frame in the same order every time – top row left to right, then bottom row. This steadiness helps them picture numbers the same way each time. After a while, cover the frame for a moment…

concepts.title: Saying, Reading and Writing 0–10
concepts.explanation: Children learn number names best through rhyme and repetition. Use the traditional counting rhyme (‘1, 2, 3, 4, 5, once I caught a fish alive…’) and hold up fingers together. Then show the written digit for each number and say its name. Writing tips: Young hands need help forming digits. Model each digit slowly and describe the movement out loud – for example, ‘2 is a curve then a line along the bottom.’ Let the child trace large digits in the air, in sand or flour, and finally on paper. Do not worry about…
concepts.example: Children learn number names best through rhyme and repetition. Use the traditional counting rhyme (‘1, 2, 3, 4, 5, once I caught a fish alive…’) and hold up fingers together. Then show the written digit for each number…

concepts.title: Estimating Then Counting
concepts.explanation: Estimation teaches children that numbers describe real amounts. Show a small handful of stones for two seconds, hide them, and ask, ‘About how many?’ Then count together to check. Praise sensible guesses, not just correct ones – the goal is a thoughtful guess, not a lucky one.
concepts.example: Estimation teaches children that numbers describe real amounts. Show a small handful of stones for two seconds, hide them, and ask, ‘About how many?’ Then count together to check. Praise sensible guesses, not just…

concepts.title: Comparing and Ordering
concepts.explanation: Lay out two groups – say 6 dates and 4 dates. Ask, ‘Which has more? Which has fewer?’ Encourage the child to check by matching one date from each group into pairs; the group with leftovers has more. This matching method is more reliable for young children than just looking, because a group of large objects can look ‘bigger’ even when it has fewer items. Then practise order: lay number cards 1–10 in a line, remove one, and ask which number is missing, using the words before, after and between. For example, ‘Which…
concepts.example: Skipping or double-counting objects. The child counts faster than they touch. Slow them down; move each counted object aside.

Build or draw a model of it.
Explain your model to a family member.
Write one question of your own and solve it.

selfAssessment: I can count a group of up to 10 objects accurately, touching each one.
I can say the number names in order from 0 to 10.
I can recognise and read the written numbers 0 to 10.
I can write the digits 0 to 10 with correct formation.
I can say how many without counting for very small groups (1–3).
I can estimate roughly how many, then count to check.
I can compare two groups and say which has more or fewer.
I can put numbers in order and find a missing number in a track.
